original_abstract: Quantifying the human perception of visual content remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision. While numerous mathematical models have been developed to measure image quality, they are often restricted to quantifying technical degradations like noise or blur and remain disconnected from natural language. Furthermore, when evaluating more abstract concepts—the "feel" rather than just the "look" of an image—researchers typically rely on supervised models trained on labor-intensive, human-labeled datasets. In this paper, we move beyond these conventional paradigms by leveraging the rich visual-language priors embedded in Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) models. We demonstrate that CLIP can be used to assess both the quality and abstract perception of images without the need for task-specific fine-tuning. By introducing optimized prompt designs and an effective prompt-pairing strategy, we show how to successfully harness these latent priors. Extensive experiments on controlled datasets and standard Image Quality Assessment (IQA) benchmarks reveal that CLIP captures sophisticated perceptual nuances that generalize across a wide range of aesthetic and technical evaluations.
humanized_abstract: Image quality assessment (IQA) is a long standing topic in computer vision. While many metrics are designed to measure image quality mathematically based on characteristics such as noise and blur, they are all shallow and do not tie the measurements to meaningful words and language. Metrics designed to measure the aesthetic feelings of an image behind its appearance need large amounts of manually annotated training data, which is very time consuming. In this work, we take a different approach based on the fact that there are many visual-language priors learned from massive datasets through pre-training, particularly Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP) models. No task specific fine-tuning is required. With a little bit of engineering including optimal prompt design and their pairwisness, we demonstrate the potential of using pre-trained CLIP for assessing both image quality and also feelings behind an image appearances. Experiments are performed on both designed controlled datasets and widely used benchmarking IQA databases, indicating the power of pre-trained CLIP models that capture a large number of more abstract and human preferences featured visual properties that can well cover a large number of different subjective aesthetic, visual comfort and quality metrics in a universal way.
